{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|= |Parham Attack| |rd| |bd|qd|kd|bd|nd|rd|= |pd|pd|pd|pd| |pd|pd|pd|= | | |nd| | | | | |= | | | | |pd| | |ql|= | | |bl| |pl| | | |= | | | | | | | | |= |pl|pl|pl|pl| |pl|pl|pl|= |rl|nl|bl| |kl| |nl|rl|= || }} =Parham Attack= ===3. Bc4=== With this aggressive move, White now threatens checkmate called "Scholar's Mate" by taking the pawn on f7 with the queen. Black has some ways to adequately prevent this mate from happening. The two decent replies are [[/3...g6|'''3...g6''']] and [[/3..Qe7|3...Qe7]]. Kasparov chose [[/3..Qe7|Qe7]] when he met this position in a game against the actor Woody Harrelson in a consultation game. At the lower level many players may be tempted to play [[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. Qh5/2...Nc6/3. Bc4/3...Nf6|3…Nf6??]] attacking White’s queen. However, Black leaves f7 undefended, and White can play the move they were intending to play when 2. Qh5 was played: 4. Qxf7#!, known as the Scholar’s Mate. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}}. '''1. e4 e5 2. Qh5 Nc6'''
